participants.name: I (0e0nbj)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-0e0nbje8
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-0e0nbje8
participants.professional_persona: I am an AI ethics researcher at a local university, focusing on algorithmic fairness and transparency for public-sector AI. I collaborate with government agencies to audit their AI systems and propose improvements.
participants.skills_and_expertise: My expertise spans fairness metrics, explainable AI, and participatory design. I have published papers on bias detection in hiring algorithms and have served on the advisory panel for NAIS 2.0.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I aim to establish a national AI ethics lab that certifies public-interest AI applications. I also want to influence international standards for responsible AI.

participants.name: A (79gpx3)
participants.professional_persona: A robotics software engineer at a smart manufacturing company, programming AI agents to coordinate autonomous guided vehicles in warehousing. He previously served as a signals technician in the military and transitioned to civilian AI roles.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expertise in ROS (Robot Operating System), sensor fusion, and path planning algorithms. Also skilled in simulation environments like Gazebo for testing before real-world deployment.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aspires to lead the AI autonomy team for warehouse robots and eventually pursue a part-time master's in AI at NUS.

participants.name: Jun (nnx9c8)
participants.professional_persona: Jun Wei L. is a Research Scientist at DSO National Laboratories, working on adversarial robustness and AI security for defence applications. He is a scholarship holder from the Singapore Armed Forces.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep expertise in adversarial machine learning, differential privacy, and secure multi-party computation. Holds a security clearance.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To advance Singapore's capabilities in trustworthy AI for national security, and to contribute to international standards on AI robustness testing.

participants.name: Mei (nbylww)
participants.professional_persona: Mei Ling K. is an independent AI researcher and consultant, advising SMEs on AI adoption and proof-of-concept projects. She retired early from a faculty position at SUTD.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Generalist with practical knowledge of ML pipelines, data engineering, and cloud deployment. She has helped 15 SMEs launch their first AI pilot.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To write a practical guide on AI adoption for Singapore's small businesses, and to volunteer as a mentor in AI apprenticeships.

participants.name: A (a4m25l)
participants.professional_persona: A mid-career convert from finance, now a junior AI researcher at a government-funded lab focused on resource-efficient AI. She is passionate about reducing the carbon footprint of large models and developing lightweight alternatives.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Proficient in model pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, and benchmarking on edge devices. Also familiar with energy-aware training techniques and AI sustainability metrics.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to publish in top-tier AI conferences on green AI and eventually launch a startup that helps SMEs deploy efficient AI on a budget.

initial_posts.narrative: As a defence AI researcher, I see NAIS 2.0's inputs as substantial but focused. The S$500M+ commitment is a solid baseline for a small nation, but I expect the actual allocation to be closer to S$500M given the policy's explicit mention. The S$330,000 cap per 100E project is realistic for industry co-funding, though defence projects often require more. Compute hours are critical; I estimate 500,000 GPU hours based on typical national AI infrastructure investments (e.g., similar to NSCC's capacity). Trainer count is modest—around 50—since the programme targets high-quality, hands-on mentorship rather than mass training, consistent with the 100E model's focus on deep collaboration.
evidence: Policy states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', so I take S$500M as the floor for total funding.
Co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated in the resource logic.

Compute infrastructure investments are implied but not quantified; based on Singapore's existing GPU clusters (e.g., NSCC's 1,000+ GPUs), 500,000 hours is a reasonable annual allocation for AI experiments.
Trainer count is not specified; given the 100E programme's project-based nature, I estimate 50 expert trainers to support ~100 projects annually, consistent with AISG's staffing scale.

initial_posts.narrative: As an independent AI consultant who has guided many SMEs through their first AI pilots, I see NAIS 2.0's inputs as ambitious but realistic for Singapore's scale. The S$500M total funding aligns with the policy's explicit commitment and my experience that government budgets for national AI strategies tend to be stated as a firm floor. The S$330,000 cap per 100E project matches what I've seen in practice—it's enough to cover a focused proof-of-concept with a small team and cloud compute, but not full-scale deployment. For compute, I estimate 500,000 GPU hours based on typical national AI infrastructure investments like NSCC's capacity; this is a reasonable starting point for experimentation. The 50 trainers figure seems low but appropriate for a high-touch mentorship model—I've seen similar ratios in apprenticeship programmes where quality trumps quantity. Jun's estimates are close to mine, and I agree with his reasoning on the focused nature of the programme.

The AI practitioner pool is growing but likely around 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, as the 120 trainees from the AI Apprenticeship Programme and other upskilling efforts add incrementally to the existing base, but scaling takes time. Enterprise AI adoption rate is around 25%, reflecting that while the 100E programme and outreach have raised awareness, many SMEs still lack the digital maturity to adopt AI solutions, consistent with my experience in defence where adoption is cautious and deliberate.
